exemplar.title: Is there a difference in neural response time, measured in milliseconds, between genders in the visual processing of a color change from red to green on a smartphone app called âReactionâ through clicking the smartphoneâs screen.

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear, focused research question situated in a well-developed physiological and literature context
Comprehensive control-variable table and detailed justification for each control
Step-by-step methodology with sample size, device details and environment specified
Effective use of Figure 1 to contextualize the visual pathway basis of the reaction-time task
weaknesses: Minor imprecision in the term âneural response timeâ; needs clearer definition of sensorimotor vs. central latency
Some background content (CNS/PNS) exceeds the scope needed for the specific research question
Key methodological considerations (trial randomization, app latency calibration) are only implicitly mentioned
Procedural ambiguities remain around wait intervals, brightness calibration and criteria for discarding practice runs
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments indicate top-band performance for question context (5) and solid descriptions of methodology and considerations (4,4). Minor errors and omissions prevent a full 6, but the overall design is both specific and reproducible with only a few ambiguities, meriting a score of 5.

strengths: Data recording and processing communicated clearly with well-labeled tables, box plot and bar graph
Effective use of both box plot and bar chart to illustrate central tendency and distribution
Calculation and depiction of error bars and standard deviations demonstrate awareness of variability
weaknesses: Significant omissions in uncertainty treatment: no estimate of app timing error or propagation of uncertainty
Inaccuracies in processed statistics (IQR mismatch, mean sums) and misuse of paired t-test for independent samples
Typographical and unit-label errors in graphs and narrative reduce precision
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication of data recording and processing meets the descriptor for âclearâ (band 4), though not precise. Consideration of uncertainties and data processing contain significant omissions and inaccuracies (band 3 each). Holistically, however, the student demonstrates clear data presentation with some processing depth, justifying a score of 4 to reflect mid-band performance and to balance total marks.

strengths: Conclusion directly addresses the research question and aligns fully with statistical results, correctly interpreting p-values
Sound consistency in distinguishing failure to reject the null hypothesis
Relevant comparisons to broader literature on gender differences in reaction time
weaknesses: Discussion of scientific context is brief and lacks critical evaluation of individual cited studies
Minor wording slips slightly reduce the polish of the justification
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both conclusion strands are at a high-band level (5,5): the student justifies conclusions consistently with analysis and uses relevant scientific context, with only minor lapses. These merits justify a score of 5 rather than 6.

strengths: Identification and description of four specific methodological weaknesses relevant to this investigation
Realistic, targeted improvements proposed for each limitation
Thorough self-evaluation showing awareness of sample, variable and device issues
weaknesses: The relative impact of each methodological weakness on validity is not fully explained
Improvements are described but lack detailed explanation of how they mitigate the specific weaknesses
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place both evaluation strands in the 3â4 band (4,4). The student describes specific limitations and realistic improvements, but does not explain their relative impact or mitigation mechanism in depth. Thus a score of 4 accurately reflects this level of critical reflection.

strandSelections.reasoning: The conclusion directly addresses the research question and is fully aligned with the statistical results presented, explicitly citing p-values and test outcomes.  The reasoning links the acceptance of the null hypothesis to insufficient evidence rather than proof of no difference, demonstrating sound consistency.  Minor wording slips exist, so a 5 (not 6) is awarded within the top band.

Raw tables, processed statistics, box plot and bar graph are all clearly laid out with titles and units, enabling the reader to follow the data trail.  Some labels contain minor typographical errors and one graph caption misstates the IQR description, slightly reducing precision but not clarity.  Overall communication is clear, though not flawlessly precise, placing it in the 3-4 band with a score of 4.

Means, medians, IQRs and a t-test are computed and related to the research question.  Nevertheless, the test selected (paired t-test) is inappropriate for two independent samples, some IQR values are reported incorrectly, and the narrative misinterprets a p-value in relation to rejecting the null.  These inaccuracies mean processing is relevant but contains notable errors, fitting the mid-band score of 3.

A dedicated table outlines four concrete methodological weaknesses (sample representativeness, uncontrolled variables, small sample size, lack of control group).  Each weakness is described with specific reference to this investigation, but the relative impact on data quality or validity is only implicitly conveyed, not fully explained.  This aligns with âdescribes specific methodological weaknessesâ, upper band 3-4.

The student compares findings to the broader literature, acknowledging typical reports of faster male reaction times and discussing overlap and confounding variables.  This comparison is relevant and used to justify the conclusion, satisfying the descriptor for a high-band performance, though the discussion remains brief and does not critically evaluate individual studies, meriting a 5 rather than a perfect 6.

The student states a clear, focused research question and situates it in a well-developed discussion of reaction-time physiology, gender findings from the literature, and the specific smartphone application to be used.  Relevant biological pathways (optic nerve, visual cortex) and previous empirical work are cited, showing that the question arises naturally from established knowledge.  Because the context is both specific (colour-change on the âReactionâ app, 16- to 17-year-olds) and appropriate to HL Biology content, the descriptor for the top band is met, though minor inaccuracies (e.g. spelling, slight digressions) justify awarding a 5 rather than a 6.

The student quotes standard deviations and depicts error bars, and briefly interprets their overlap.  However, measurement uncertainty of the smartphone timer, reaction-time resolution or sampling error is not estimated; no propagation of uncertainty is carried out.  Thus uncertainties are considered, but with significant omissions, warranting the lower half of the 3-4 band.

For every listed limitation the student proposes a corresponding, realistic improvement (e.g., broaden age range, quantify prior experience, increase trials).  While the suggestions are relevant, the explanation of how each change would mitigate the identified weakness is concise rather than elaborated, meeting the descriptor for âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ improvements.  Hence a score of 4 in the 3-4 band is appropriate.

A comprehensive set of variables is presented in tabular form, with explanations for how each will be controlled and why it matters.  Sample size, environment, device standardisation and timing are considered, demonstrating awareness of data relevance and sufficiency.  However, several key methodological issues (e.g. randomisation of trial order, latency of the app, individual calibration) are not addressed or are only implicitly mentioned, so the considerations are better described than explained.  This fits the 3-4 band, at the top of that range.

The step-by-step procedure (points 1â13) together with the control tables gives enough detail for another investigator to repeat the study: participant number, device model, number of trials, environment and data-recording method are all stated.  Nonetheless, some ambiguities remain (exact wait interval before stimulus, calibration of brightness, criteria for discarding practice runs), so reproduction would require clarification.  This corresponds to âfew ambiguities or omissionsâ, hence a high score in the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The research question is clear but the term âneural response timeâ could be misinterpreted; specify whether you mean total sensorimotor reaction time or central processing latency to improve precision.
The detailed description of the CNS and PNS overshoots the scope needed; focus background on the visual pathway components directly relevant to reaction measurement.
The inclusion of Figure 1 showing the optic nerve and visual pathway effectively contextualizes the physiological basis of the task.
The citation âReaction Time,â n.d. lacks retrieval date or source details; ensure full APAâstyle references for all background sources.
Hypothesis rationale relies heavily on general brain size differences; strengthen by citing studies quantifying neural conduction velocities or synaptic processing speed.
The controlâvariable table omits trial randomization or counterbalancing; include this to reduce order effects across participants.
Controlling collection time to 12:00 Â± 1 hr ignores individual circadian differences; consider chronotype screening or tighter time windows.
The unit âmilisecondsâ is misspelt; correct to âmillisecondsâ for clarity and consistency in methodology.
Uncontrolled variables table lists motivation but offers no mitigation; include a validated questionnaire to quantify and control this factor.
Specify demographic recording as its own step (e.g. âStep 8: Record age, gender, handedness and prior experienceâ) to avoid procedural ambiguity.
The appâs built-in timer latency is not calibrated; recommend benchmarking timing against a standard stopwatch to estimate systematic error.
Clearly stating sample size and gender balance (11 males, 11 females) in Step 1 enhances reproducibility and transparency.
Raw data tables lack notation of excluded outliers; indicate which data points were removed and justify their exclusion for transparency.
The narrative states female IQR > male IQR, but calculations show male IQR (46 ms) > female IQR (27 ms); correct this label inconsistency.
Processed summary table lists female maximum as 452 ms, but raw data show 515 ms; recheck calculations to resolve this discrepancy.
The cleaned table of quartiles and extremes effectively summarizes distribution comparisons for both groups.
Box plot description uses â411sâ and â376sâ inadvertently implying seconds; revise to â411 msâ and â376 msâ for unit consistency.
Mean calculation sums (4450, 4193) do not match raw totals; recalculate group sums to confirm accurate means.
The narrative on hypothesis testing misstates rejection rules (âreject null due to insufficient evidenceâ); clarify that p>0.05 means fail to reject or accept the null.
Using both box plot and bar chart representations strengthens clarity of data distribution and central tendency.
Standard deviation uses N in denominator rather than Nâ1; justify sample vs population SD choice or adjust for unbiased estimate.
Interpretation of overlapping error bars is qualitative; include confidence intervals or numerical overlap to quantify uncertainty.
The terminology confuses IQR and SD when discussing variability; ensure precise use of statistical terms.
A paired t-test is inappropriate for two independent groups; use an independent two-sample t-test to match your design.
The text states âreject null due to insufficient evidenceâ despite p>0.05; correct phrasing to âfail to rejectâ the null hypothesis.
Qualitative observation of practice effects is interesting but would be strengthened by statistical correlation analysis between trial number and reaction time.
The conclusion directly aligns with statistical results and correctly acknowledges evidence limitations, demonstrating consistency.
The broader context discussion cites general trends but lacks critique of key studies; add detailed comparisons to strengthen scientific justification.
The strengths table omits discussion of device latency calibration; include this methodological aspect when evaluating rigor.
Limitations table improvements list generic suggestions but do not explain how each addresses the specific weakness; elaborate causal linkage.
Identification of sample representativeness, variable scope and sample size as limitations shows thorough selfâevaluation.

The processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out appropriately and accurately.
